text: Effect of the pending separation: a contested separation that has not been pronounced is extinguished by the husband's death, so the spouses are still married at the opening of the succession; the wife is a forced heir. (Only a final separation pronounced against her with a charge of fault would reduce her rights to a life annuity under Art. 548 CC.) Property regime: under the separation-of-property regime (Art. 215 CC) there is no community to divide, so the whole of the husband's own assets forms the relictum - no half is split off for the wife. Reserved share: the spouse not concurring with children is reserved 1/2 of the estate, with 1/2 disposable (Art. 540 CC). Remedies: the holographic will is valid if it meets Art. 602 CC; the wife may both contest its formal validity (Art. 606 CC) and, recovering her legittima, bring the action for reduction against the third-party heir (Arts. 553 ff. CC).

A holographic will is valid only if it satisfies the three cumulative requirements of Art. 602 CC: (1) autografia - it must be written entirely in the testator's own hand; (2) the date - day, month and year; and (3) the testator's signature (sottoscrizione) at the end, sufficient to identify him. The testator must also have testamentary capacity (Art. 591 CC). Lack of handwriting or of the signature makes the will null; defects in the date or other formalities make it merely annullable on action of an interested party within five years (Art. 606 CC).

Yes. Under Art. 603 CC the public will (testamento pubblico) is received by a notary in the continuous presence of two witnesses. The testator declares his wishes orally to the notary, who reduces them to writing and reads the act back to the testator in the witnesses' presence; mention of compliance with these formalities must appear in the deed, which is signed by testator, witnesses and notary. The two witnesses are an essential, non-waivable formality, so a public will always requires them.

Yes. Under Art. 684 CC the total or partial destruction, cancellation or obliteration of a holographic will, carried out by the testator or with his knowledge, produces revocation, unless it is shown that the destruction was the work of a third party, accidental, or done without revocatory intent (animus revocandi). Precautions: the act must be performed by the testator with clear intent to revoke; every original/copy should be destroyed to avoid reconstruction; and, to forestall disputes, revocation is more safely made by a later will (Art. 681 CC) or by an express revocation declared before a notary and two witnesses (Art. 680 CC).

Validity of the reciprocal will: Art. 589 CC prohibits joint and reciprocal wills - two persons cannot test in the same document, whether for a third party or reciprocally; such a will is void. If instead each spouse made a separate mirror will, each is individually valid. The succession provisions agreed in the assisted negotiation are also void as succession agreements (Art. 458 CC). Effect of separation: a consensual separation without a charge of fault does not remove spousal succession rights - the separated, non-faulted spouse inherits as a spouse (Art. 548 CC) - so the surviving spouse remains a forced heir. Reserved shares with spouse + one child: the child is reserved 1/3 and the spouse 1/3, leaving 1/3 disposable (Art. 542, para. 1 CC); the minor's reserved third is claimed through the tutor, with acceptance by benefit of inventory and the usual authorisations.

A child is a forced heir (legittimario) entitled to a reserved share (Arts. 536 ff. CC). A mere testamentary clause excluding a child (diseredazione) does not deprive him of the legittima: the child can still claim it through the action for reduction, and any provision burdening the reserved share is void (Art. 549 CC). Italian case law (e.g. Cass. 8352/2012) admits disinheritance as an implicit disposition, but only of the disposable quota - it can never defeat the reserved share. A child can be wholly excluded from the succession only where he is declared unworthy (indegnita) on the limited grounds of Art. 463 CC, such as killing or attempting to kill the deceased or serious offences against him.

Framework: (1) The undissolved legal community with the late husband must be liquidated first (Art. 191 CC): the husband's half already devolved through his succession; the mother held her half, so on her death her half of the former community assets plus her own assets form the relictum. (2) Intestate, the three children take equal shares of 1/3 each (Art. 566 CC). (3) For each minor, acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC); each tutor separately petitions the tutelary judge for authorisation to accept (Art. 374, no. 3 CC), and a special curator is appointed where the two minors' interests conflict (Art. 320, last para. CC). (4) The division accounts for collation among the children (Arts. 737 ff. CC); assisted negotiation or mediation - mandatory as a condition of admissibility for hereditary division and successions (Art. 5 D.Lgs. 28/2010) - may resolve the tutors' disputes, but any settlement affecting the minors still requires approval by the tutelary judge.

A specific legacy (legato di specie, Art. 649 CC) has real effect: ownership of the determinate thing belonging to the testator passes to the legatee automatically at the opening of the succession, and the risk of loss falls on the legatee. A generic legacy (legato di genere or di quantita, Art. 653 CC) has only obligational effect: the burdened heir must deliver or procure a thing of the indicated kind; ownership passes only upon individualisation/delivery, and until then the risk lies with the heir. Choice of the thing, absent contrary will, belongs to the obligor who must give a thing of average quality (Art. 664 CC).

The secret will (testamento segreto) combines a written sheet and an act of reception. Form (Art. 604 CC): it may be written by the testator or by a third party, by hand or by mechanical means; if not entirely handwritten and signed by the testator, he must sign each half-sheet; the paper is then sealed so that it cannot be opened or removed without breaking the seal. Reception (Art. 605 CC): the testator personally delivers the sealed will to a notary in the presence of two witnesses, declaring it contains his will; the notary writes the act of reception on the wrapper or envelope, stating the formalities, and it is signed by testator, witnesses and notary. The testator may withdraw it at any time (Art. 608 CC); if it lacks a formality of the secret will but meets the requirements of a holographic will, it is valid as such (Art. 607 CC).

Where the deceased leaves no descendants, no spouse and no ascendants, the estate passes to the siblings under Art. 570 CC. Two surviving siblings therefore take the whole estate in equal shares (1/2 each). Half-blood siblings (unilaterali) receive one half of the share of full-blood siblings (Art. 570, para. 2 CC). If a parent or ascendant were alive, the siblings would instead concur with them under Art. 571 CC, but here, with only the two siblings, the split is equal.

Pending-separation status: the personal separation proceeding is extinguished by the death of a party (the matrimonial bond ends by death, not by the abated action), so the spouses are deemed still married at the opening of the succession and the surviving wife retains full spousal succession rights. Hereditary mass: death dissolves the legal community (Art. 191 CC); the wife takes 1/2 of the community assets iure proprio, and the husband's 1/2 plus his personal assets (Art. 179 CC), net of debts, forms the relictum. Intestate shares with spouse + two children: spouse 1/3, children 2/3 (Art. 581 CC). Protection of the minors: acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC); the surviving parent/tutor must obtain authorisation from the tutelary judge to accept (Art. 320 CC), and where her interest conflicts with the children's a special curator is appointed (Art. 320, last para. CC; Art. 78 CPC). These authorisations proceed in voluntary jurisdiction.

Framework: (1) The undissolved legal community with the late husband must be liquidated first (Art. 191 CC): the husband's half already devolved through his succession; the mother held her half, so on her death her half of the former community assets plus her own assets form the relictum. (2) Intestate, the three children take equal shares of 1/3 each (Art. 566 CC). (3) For each minor, acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC); each tutor separately petitions the tutelary judge for authorisation to accept (Art. 374, no. 3 CC), and a special curator is appointed where the two minors' interests conflict (Art. 320, last para. CC). (4) The division accounts for collation among the children (Arts. 737 ff. CC); assisted negotiation or mediation - mandatory as a condition of admissibility for hereditary division and successions (Art. 5 D.Lgs. 28/2010) - may resolve the tutors' disputes, but any settlement affecting the minors still requires approval by the tutelary judge.

In the absence of a will, succession devolves by law to the categories of Art. 565 CC, in order: spouse, descendants, ascendants, collaterals, other relatives up to the sixth degree, and finally the State. Main shares: children alone inherit in equal parts (Art. 566 CC); spouse and one child take one half each, while spouse and two or more children take 1/3 to the spouse and 2/3 to the children (Art. 581 CC); spouse concurring with ascendants and/or siblings takes 2/3, the rest 1/3 (Art. 582 CC); the spouse alone inherits the whole estate (Art. 583 CC). The spouse also enjoys the rights of habitation and use under Art. 540 CC.

The spouse also enjoys the rights of habitation and use under Art. 540 CC.

If an heir cannot or does not wish to accept - for example because he predeceased the deceased, is unworthy, or renounces - the doctrine of representation (Arts. 467-469 CC) allows his descendants to take his place and degree in the succession. Representation operates ad infinitum in favour of the descendants of the deceased's children and of the descendants of the deceased's siblings (Art. 468 CC). Division is per stirpes: the representatives share among themselves the single portion that the represented person would have taken (Art. 469 CC).

Procedural sequence: (1) Because the legal community with the predeceased wife was never dissolved, it must first be liquidated (Art. 191 CC): the wife's half already devolved through her own succession, so the co-owned real estate is settled and apportioned before the father's estate is divided. (2) The father's succession opens; the two minor children are his sole intestate heirs in equal shares (Art. 566 CC). (3) A tutor is appointed for the minors by the tutelary judge, both parents being deceased (Arts. 343, 346 CC). (4) Acceptance for the minors is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC). (5) The tutor petitions the tutelary judge for authorisation to accept with benefit of inventory (Art. 374, no. 3 CC) - voluntary jurisdiction. (6) The inventory is drawn up (Art. 484 CC). (7) Any subsequent act of extraordinary administration on the children's real estate requires further authorisation (Art. 375 CC).

Acceptance with benefit of inventory is made by a declaration received by a notary or by the clerk of the court of the place where the succession opened, recorded in the succession register and transcribed (Art. 484 CC). An inventory must be drawn up within the statutory terms: an heir in possession of estate assets must make the inventory within three months of the opening (then has forty days to decide), while an heir not in possession may declare first and complete the inventory within set terms (Arts. 485-487 CC). The effect (Art. 490 CC) is the separation of the heir's own patrimony from that of the deceased, so the heir answers for hereditary debts only within the value of what he receives (intra vires hereditatis). For minors and other incapables acceptance is always with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC) and requires authorisation of the tutelary judge.

Acceptance with benefit of inventory is made by a declaration received by a notary or by the clerk of the court of the place where the succession opened, recorded in the succession register and transcribed (Art. 484 CC). An inventory must be drawn up within the statutory terms: an heir in possession of estate assets must make the inventory within three months of the opening (then has forty days to decide), while an heir not in possession may declare first and complete the inventory within set terms (Arts. 485-487 CC). The effect (Art. 490 CC) is the separation of the heir's own patrimony from that of the deceased, so the heir answers for hereditary debts only within the value of what he receives (intra vires hereditatis). For minors and other incapables acceptance is always with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC) and requires authorisation of the tutelary judge.

Steps: (1) Note that succession agreements are void (Art. 458 CC), so the 'mutual succession provisions' inserted in the assisted-negotiation agreement cannot bind the future succession; they have no effect on the devolution. (2) On the wife's intestate death the minor child is sole heir (Art. 566 CC); the separated/ex-spouse is not an heir. (3) Because the inheritance is burdened with debts, the protective course is acceptance with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC) to cap liability intra vires; outright renunciation for the minor is possible but only on the child's interest. (4) The father, exercising parental responsibility/as tutor, files a petition with the tutelary judge (voluntary jurisdiction) for authorisation to renounce (Arts. 320 and 374, no. 3 CC), showing the renunciation serves the minor's interest. (5) If authorised, the renunciation is declared before the notary or court clerk and entered in the succession register (Art. 519 CC).

Renunciation of an inheritance must be made by an express declaration received by a notary or by the clerk of the court of the place where the succession opened, and entered in the succession register (Art. 519 CC); it cannot be partial, conditional or subject to a term (Art. 520 CC). Effect: the renouncer is treated as never having been called, and his share accrues to the other heirs or passes by representation or substitution (Art. 522 CC). The right to accept or renounce is subject to a ten-year limitation period (Art. 480 CC); renunciation may be revoked so long as the right has not prescribed and no other entitled person has accepted (Art. 525 CC). Creditors of the renouncer prejudiced by the renunciation may obtain authorisation to accept the inheritance in his name, within five years, to satisfy their claims (Art. 524 CC).

Death dissolves the legal community of property (Art. 191 CC). The surviving spouse (your mother) first takes, in her own right and not as inheritance, one half of the assets that fell into the community under Art. 177 CC; the deceased father's half of the community assets, together with his personal/separate assets (Art. 179 CC), forms the relictum. The hereditary mass to be divided is therefore: father's 1/2 of the community assets + his personal assets, less the hereditary debts; for the purpose of the reserved shares this relictum is then notionally augmented by the value of his lifetime donations (riunione fittizia, Art. 556 CC). The heirs (intestate) are the mother and the two children, who take respectively 1/3 and 2/3 under Art. 581 CC, unless a will provides otherwise.

Treatment of the conversion: a convention changing the matrimonial property regime is made by public deed before a notary (Arts. 162-163 CC) and, dissolving the legal community (Art. 191 CC), splits the community assets - including the family business - in equal halves between the spouses at the moment of conversion. Succession: at death the regime is separation of property, so the estate comprises only the deceased's own assets (his half of the former business and his other assets). The will may freely allocate the shares within the disposable quota, but the forced heirs (spouse and children) may raise the action for reduction (Arts. 553 ff. CC) if their reserved shares (Arts. 537 and 542 CC) are impaired; the reserve is computed on the relictum net of debts plus the notional reunion of donations (Art. 556 CC). A neutral 50/50 split on conversion is not a donation, but any conversion that disguised a liberality could itself be drawn into that computation.

Procedural framework: each heir decides independently, so divergent choices are admissible. For each minor acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC), and the tutor needs authorisation of the tutelary judge to accept (Art. 374, no. 3 CC) or, in the second minor's case, to renounce (Arts. 320 and 374 CC); the renouncing minor's share accrues to the coheirs or passes by representation/substitution (Art. 522 CC). The adult heir may demand collation in the division: descendants and the spouse who accept must confer the donations received (Arts. 737 ff. CC). Sequence: the authorisations are obtained in voluntary jurisdiction; the estate is then divided - amicably, or by judicial division (Arts. 784 ff. CPC) if there is no agreement - with the collation account settled; the minors are represented by their tutors, with a special curator where their interests conflict (Art. 320, last para. CC).

Collation (Arts. 737 ff. CC) is the obligation of children and other descendants and of the spouse who accept the inheritance to return (conferire) to the estate the donations they received from the deceased during his life, so that the relictum and the donatum are divided proportionally and equality among the coheirs is preserved. It can be made in kind or by imputation - for immovables at the election of the donee (Art. 746 CC), for movables by imputation (Art. 750 CC); for money see Art. 751 CC. Subject to collation are direct and indirect donations. Excluded are expenses of maintenance, education and customary or modest gifts (Art. 742 CC), and donations for which the testator expressly granted a dispensation from collation, valid within the disposable quota (Art. 737, para. 1 CC).

Collation (Arts. 737 ff. CC) is the obligation of children and other descendants and of the spouse who accept the inheritance to return (conferire) to the estate the donations they received from the deceased during his life, so that the relictum and the donatum are divided proportionally and equality among the coheirs is preserved. It can be made in kind or by imputation - for immovables at the election of the donee (Art. 746 CC), for movables by imputation (Art. 750 CC); for money see Art. 751 CC. Subject to collation are direct and indirect donations. Excluded are expenses of maintenance, education and customary or modest gifts (Art. 742 CC), and donations for which the testator expressly granted a dispensation from collation, valid within the disposable quota (Art. 737, para. 1 CC).

Collation (Arts. 737 ff. CC) is the obligation of children and other descendants and of the spouse who accept the inheritance to return (conferire) to the estate the donations they received from the deceased during his life, so that the relictum and the donatum are divided proportionally and equality among the coheirs is preserved. It can be made in kind or by imputation - for immovables at the election of the donee (Art. 746 CC), for movables by imputation (Art. 750 CC); for money see Art. 751 CC. Subject to collation are direct and indirect donations. Excluded are expenses of maintenance, education and customary or modest gifts (Art. 742 CC), and donations for which the testator expressly granted a dispensation from collation, valid within the disposable quota (Art. 737, para. 1 CC).

Assignment of the family home: the home had been assigned to the wife in the interest of the children (Art. 337-sexies CC); on her death that assignment ends, and the children inherit her ownership share of the home as her sole/principal heirs (Art. 566 CC). Parental authority and guardianship: the surviving father in principle resumes exclusive parental responsibility (Arts. 316-317 CC); where he is to be appointed tutor (e.g. he had been excluded, or a conflict exists) the tutelary judge makes the appointment (Arts. 343, 346 CC). Management of the inherited property: acts of extraordinary administration on the minors' real estate - sale, mortgage, lease over nine years - require authorisation of the tutelary judge (Arts. 320, para. 3, 374 and 375 CC), in voluntary jurisdiction. The earlier mediated separation settlement governs the separation only and does not bind the succession.

Pending-separation status: the personal separation proceeding is extinguished by the death of a party (the matrimonial bond ends by death, not by the abated action), so the spouses are deemed still married at the opening of the succession and the surviving wife retains full spousal succession rights. Hereditary mass: death dissolves the legal community (Art. 191 CC); the wife takes 1/2 of the community assets iure proprio, and the husband's 1/2 plus his personal assets (Art. 179 CC), net of debts, forms the relictum. Intestate shares with spouse + two children: spouse 1/3, children 2/3 (Art. 581 CC). Protection of the minors: acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC); the surviving parent/tutor must obtain authorisation from the tutelary judge to accept (Art. 320 CC), and where her interest conflicts with the children's a special curator is appointed (Art. 320, last para. CC; Art. 78 CPC). These authorisations proceed in voluntary jurisdiction.

Reserved portion: with the spouse concurring with two (or more) children, the children are reserved 1/2 collectively and the spouse 1/4, leaving 1/4 disposable (Art. 542, para. 2 CC). Computation: take the relictum, deduct the debts, and add back (riunione fittizia) the value of the deceased's lifetime donations (Art. 556 CC); the reserved quotas are computed on this notional mass. Remedy: the forced heirs bring the action for reduction against the foundation (Arts. 553-564 CC), first reducing the testamentary disposition and then, if necessary, the donations, with restitution of the assets needed to fill the reserve (Art. 561 CC); the action is subject to the ten-year limitation. Role of mediation: disputes over hereditary division and successions are subject to mandatory mediation as a condition of admissibility of the claim (Art. 5 D.Lgs. 28/2010); family mediation/assisted negotiation can produce a settlement among the heirs and the foundation, but it cannot extinguish a forced heir's reserved right unless that heir consents.

Framework: (1) The undissolved legal community with the late husband must be liquidated first (Art. 191 CC): the husband's half already devolved through his succession; the mother held her half, so on her death her half of the former community assets plus her own assets form the relictum. (2) Intestate, the three children take equal shares of 1/3 each (Art. 566 CC). (3) For each minor, acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC); each tutor separately petitions the tutelary judge for authorisation to accept (Art. 374, no. 3 CC), and a special curator is appointed where the two minors' interests conflict (Art. 320, last para. CC). (4) The division accounts for collation among the children (Arts. 737 ff. CC); assisted negotiation or mediation - mandatory as a condition of admissibility for hereditary division and successions (Art. 5 D.Lgs. 28/2010) - may resolve the tutors' disputes, but any settlement affecting the minors still requires approval by the tutelary judge.

Procedural sequence: (1) Because the legal community with the predeceased wife was never dissolved, it must first be liquidated (Art. 191 CC): the wife's half already devolved through her own succession, so the co-owned real estate is settled and apportioned before the father's estate is divided. (2) The father's succession opens; the two minor children are his sole intestate heirs in equal shares (Art. 566 CC). (3) A tutor is appointed for the minors by the tutelary judge, both parents being deceased (Arts. 343, 346 CC). (4) Acceptance for the minors is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC). (5) The tutor petitions the tutelary judge for authorisation to accept with benefit of inventory (Art. 374, no. 3 CC) - voluntary jurisdiction. (6) The inventory is drawn up (Art. 484 CC). (7) Any subsequent act of extraordinary administration on the children's real estate requires further authorisation (Art. 375 CC).

(2) Intestate, the three children take equal shares of 1/3 each (Art. 566 CC). Effect of the pending separation: a contested separation that has not been pronounced is extinguished by the husband's death, so the spouses are still married at the opening of the succession; the wife is a forced heir. (Only a final separation pronounced against her with a charge of fault would reduce her rights to a life annuity under Art. 548 CC.) Property regime: under the separation-of-property regime (Art. 215 CC) there is no community to divide, so the whole of the husband's own assets forms the relictum - no half is split off for the wife. Reserved share: the spouse not concurring with children is reserved 1/2 of the estate, with 1/2 disposable (Art. 540 CC). Remedies: the holographic will is valid if it meets Art. 602 CC; the wife may both contest its formal validity (Art. 606 CC) and, recovering her legittima, bring the action for reduction against the third-party heir (Arts. 553 ff. CC).

Validity of the reciprocal will: Art. 589 CC prohibits joint and reciprocal wills - two persons cannot test in the same document, whether for a third party or reciprocally; such a will is void. If instead each spouse made a separate mirror will, each is individually valid. The succession provisions agreed in the assisted negotiation are also void as succession agreements (Art. 458 CC). Effect of separation: a consensual separation without a charge of fault does not remove spousal succession rights - the separated, non-faulted spouse inherits as a spouse (Art. 548 CC) - so the surviving spouse remains a forced heir. Reserved shares with spouse + one child: the child is reserved 1/3 and the spouse 1/3, leaving 1/3 disposable (Art. 542, para. 1 CC); the minor's reserved third is claimed through the tutor, with acceptance by benefit of inventory and the usual authorisations.

Treatment of the conversion: a convention changing the matrimonial property regime is made by public deed before a notary (Arts. 162-163 CC) and, dissolving the legal community (Art. 191 CC), splits the community assets - including the family business - in equal halves between the spouses at the moment of conversion. Succession: at death the regime is separation of property, so the estate comprises only the deceased's own assets (his half of the former business and his other assets). The will may freely allocate the shares within the disposable quota, but the forced heirs (spouse and children) may raise the action for reduction (Arts. 553 ff. CC) if their reserved shares (Arts. 537 and 542 CC) are impaired; the reserve is computed on the relictum net of debts plus the notional reunion of donations (Art. 556 CC). A neutral 50/50 split on conversion is not a donation, but any conversion that disguised a liberality could itself be drawn into that computation.

Procedural framework: each heir decides independently, so divergent choices are admissible. For each minor acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC), and the tutor needs authorisation of the tutelary judge to accept (Art. 374, no. 3 CC) or, in the second minor's case, to renounce (Arts. 320 and 374 CC); the renouncing minor's share accrues to the coheirs or passes by representation/substitution (Art. 522 CC). The adult heir may demand collation in the division: descendants and the spouse who accept must confer the donations received (Arts. 737 ff. CC). Sequence: the authorisations are obtained in voluntary jurisdiction; the estate is then divided - amicably, or by judicial division (Arts. 784 ff. CPC) if there is no agreement - with the collation account settled; the minors are represented by their tutors, with a special curator where their interests conflict (Art. 320, last para. CC).

A holographic will is valid only if it satisfies the three cumulative requirements of Art. 602 CC: (1) autografia - it must be written entirely in the testator's own hand; (2) the date - day, month and year; and (3) the testator's signature (sottoscrizione) at the end, sufficient to identify him. The testator must also have testamentary capacity (Art. 591 CC). Lack of handwriting or of the signature makes the will null; defects in the date or other formalities make it merely annullable on action of an interested party within five years (Art. 606 CC).

The secret will (testamento segreto) combines a written sheet and an act of reception. Form (Art. 604 CC): it may be written by the testator or by a third party, by hand or by mechanical means; if not entirely handwritten and signed by the testator, he must sign each half-sheet; the paper is then sealed so that it cannot be opened or removed without breaking the seal. Reception (Art. 605 CC): the testator personally delivers the sealed will to a notary in the presence of two witnesses, declaring it contains his will; the notary writes the act of reception on the wrapper or envelope, stating the formalities, and it is signed by testator, witnesses and notary. The testator may withdraw it at any time (Art. 608 CC); if it lacks a formality of the secret will but meets the requirements of a holographic will, it is valid as such (Art. 607 CC).

Yes. Under Art. 684 CC the total or partial destruction, cancellation or obliteration of a holographic will, carried out by the testator or with his knowledge, produces revocation, unless it is shown that the destruction was the work of a third party, accidental, or done without revocatory intent (animus revocandi). Precautions: the act must be performed by the testator with clear intent to revoke; every original/copy should be destroyed to avoid reconstruction; and, to forestall disputes, revocation is more safely made by a later will (Art. 681 CC) or by an express revocation declared before a notary and two witnesses (Art. 680 CC).

Yes. Under Art. 603 CC the public will (testamento pubblico) is received by a notary in the continuous presence of two witnesses. The testator declares his wishes orally to the notary, who reduces them to writing and reads the act back to the testator in the witnesses' presence; mention of compliance with these formalities must appear in the deed, which is signed by testator, witnesses and notary. The two witnesses are an essential, non-waivable formality, so a public will always requires them.

The will is valid but reducible. A testator may dispose only of the disposable quota; here the spouse concurs with three (more than two) children, so Art. 542, para. 2 CC reserves 1/2 to the children and 1/4 to the spouse, leaving only 1/4 disposable. The institution of the third party is not void: it is effective for the disposable 1/4, while the disposition exceeding it injures the reserved share and is subject to the action for reduction (azione di riduzione, Arts. 553-564 CC) brought by the forced heirs. Any clause burdening or conditioning the legittima is ineffective (Art. 549 CC). For the minor children, acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC) and requires authorisation of the tutelary judge.

A specific legacy (legato di specie, Art. 649 CC) has real effect: ownership of the determinate thing belonging to the testator passes to the legatee automatically at the opening of the succession, and the risk of loss falls on the legatee. A generic legacy (legato di genere or di quantita, Art. 653 CC) has only obligational effect: the burdened heir must deliver or procure a thing of the indicated kind; ownership passes only upon individualisation/delivery, and until then the risk lies with the heir. Choice of the thing, absent contrary will, belongs to the obligor who must give a thing of average quality (Art. 664 CC).

A child is a forced heir (legittimario) entitled to a reserved share (Arts. 536 ff. CC). A mere testamentary clause excluding a child (diseredazione) does not deprive him of the legittima: the child can still claim it through the action for reduction, and any provision burdening the reserved share is void (Art. 549 CC). Italian case law (e.g. Cass. 8352/2012) admits disinheritance as an implicit disposition, but only of the disposable quota - it can never defeat the reserved share. A child can be wholly excluded from the succession only where he is declared unworthy (indegnita) on the limited grounds of Art. 463 CC, such as killing or attempting to kill the deceased or serious offences against him.

In the absence of a will, succession devolves by law to the categories of Art. 565 CC, in order: spouse, descendants, ascendants, collaterals, other relatives up to the sixth degree, and finally the State. Main shares: children alone inherit in equal parts (Art. 566 CC); spouse and one child take one half each, while spouse and two or more children take 1/3 to the spouse and 2/3 to the children (Art. 581 CC); spouse concurring with ascendants and/or siblings takes 2/3, the rest 1/3 (Art. 582 CC); the spouse alone inherits the whole estate (Art. 583 CC). The spouse also enjoys the rights of habitation and use under Art. 540 CC.

In the absence of a will, succession devolves by law to the categories of Art. 565 CC, in order: spouse, descendants, ascendants, collaterals, other relatives up to the sixth degree, and finally the State. Main shares: children alone inherit in equal parts (Art. 566 CC); spouse and one child take one half each, while spouse and two or more children take 1/3 to the spouse and 2/3 to the children (Art. 581 CC); spouse concurring with ascendants and/or siblings takes 2/3, the rest 1/3 (Art. 582 CC); the spouse alone inherits the whole estate (Art. 583 CC). The spouse also enjoys the rights of habitation and use under Art. 540 CC.

Collation (Arts. 737 ff. CC) is the obligation of children and other descendants and of the spouse who accept the inheritance to return (conferire) to the estate the donations they received from the deceased during his life, so that the relictum and the donatum are divided proportionally and equality among the coheirs is preserved. It can be made in kind or by imputation - for immovables at the election of the donee (Art. 746 CC), for movables by imputation (Art. 750 CC); for money see Art. 751 CC. Subject to collation are direct and indirect donations. Excluded are expenses of maintenance, education and customary or modest gifts (Art. 742 CC), and donations for which the testator expressly granted a dispensation from collation, valid within the disposable quota (Art. 737, para. 1 CC).

Where the deceased leaves no descendants, no spouse and no ascendants, the estate passes to the siblings under Art. 570 CC. Two surviving siblings therefore take the whole estate in equal shares (1/2 each). Half-blood siblings (unilaterali) receive one half of the share of full-blood siblings (Art. 570, para. 2 CC). If a parent or ascendant were alive, the siblings would instead concur with them under Art. 571 CC, but here, with only the two siblings, the split is equal.

If an heir cannot or does not wish to accept - for example because he predeceased the deceased, is unworthy, or renounces - the doctrine of representation (Arts. 467-469 CC) allows his descendants to take his place and degree in the succession. Representation operates ad infinitum in favour of the descendants of the deceased's children and of the descendants of the deceased's siblings (Art. 468 CC). Division is per stirpes: the representatives share among themselves the single portion that the represented person would have taken (Art. 469 CC).

Renunciation of an inheritance must be made by an express declaration received by a notary or by the clerk of the court of the place where the succession opened, and entered in the succession register (Art. 519 CC); it cannot be partial, conditional or subject to a term (Art. 520 CC). Effect: the renouncer is treated as never having been called, and his share accrues to the other heirs or passes by representation or substitution (Art. 522 CC). The right to accept or renounce is subject to a ten-year limitation period (Art. 480 CC); renunciation may be revoked so long as the right has not prescribed and no other entitled person has accepted (Art. 525 CC). Creditors of the renouncer prejudiced by the renunciation may obtain authorisation to accept the inheritance in his name, within five years, to satisfy their claims (Art. 524 CC).

Acceptance with benefit of inventory is made by a declaration received by a notary or by the clerk of the court of the place where the succession opened, recorded in the succession register and transcribed (Art. 484 CC). An inventory must be drawn up within the statutory terms: an heir in possession of estate assets must make the inventory within three months of the opening (then has forty days to decide), while an heir not in possession may declare first and complete the inventory within set terms (Arts. 485-487 CC). The effect (Art. 490 CC) is the separation of the heir's own patrimony from that of the deceased, so the heir answers for hereditary debts only within the value of what he receives (intra vires hereditatis). For minors and other incapables acceptance is always with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC) and requires authorisation of the tutelary judge.

Death dissolves the legal community of property (Art. 191 CC). The surviving spouse (your mother) first takes, in her own right and not as inheritance, one half of the assets that fell into the community under Art. 177 CC; the deceased father's half of the community assets, together with his personal/separate assets (Art. 179 CC), forms the relictum. The hereditary mass to be divided is therefore: father's 1/2 of the community assets + his personal assets, less the hereditary debts; for the purpose of the reserved shares this relictum is then notionally augmented by the value of his lifetime donations (riunione fittizia, Art. 556 CC). The heirs (intestate) are the mother and the two children, who take respectively 1/3 and 2/3 under Art. 581 CC, unless a will provides otherwise.

Pending-separation status: the personal separation proceeding is extinguished by the death of a party (the matrimonial bond ends by death, not by the abated action), so the spouses are deemed still married at the opening of the succession and the surviving wife retains full spousal succession rights. Hereditary mass: death dissolves the legal community (Art. 191 CC); the wife takes 1/2 of the community assets iure proprio, and the husband's 1/2 plus his personal assets (Art. 179 CC), net of debts, forms the relictum. Intestate shares with spouse + two children: spouse 1/3, children 2/3 (Art. 581 CC). Protection of the minors: acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC); the surviving parent/tutor must obtain authorisation from the tutelary judge to accept (Art. 320 CC), and where her interest conflicts with the children's a special curator is appointed (Art. 320, last para. CC; Art. 78 CPC). These authorisations proceed in voluntary jurisdiction.

Framework: (1) The undissolved legal community with the late husband must be liquidated first (Art. 191 CC): the husband's half already devolved through his succession; the mother held her half, so on her death her half of the former community assets plus her own assets form the relictum. (2) Intestate, the three children take equal shares of 1/3 each (Art. 566 CC). (3) For each minor, acceptance is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC); each tutor separately petitions the tutelary judge for authorisation to accept (Art. 374, no. 3 CC), and a special curator is appointed where the two minors' interests conflict (Art. 320, last para. CC). (4) The division accounts for collation among the children (Arts. 737 ff. CC); assisted negotiation or mediation - mandatory as a condition of admissibility for hereditary division and successions (Art. 5 D.Lgs. 28/2010) - may resolve the tutors' disputes, but any settlement affecting the minors still requires approval by the tutelary judge.

CC; Art. 78 CPC). These authorisations proceed in voluntary jurisdiction.

Assignment of the family home: the home had been assigned to the wife in the interest of the children (Art. 337-sexies CC); on her death that assignment ends, and the children inherit her ownership share of the home as her sole/principal heirs (Art. 566 CC). Parental authority and guardianship: the surviving father in principle resumes exclusive parental responsibility (Arts. 316-317 CC); where he is to be appointed tutor (e.g. he had been excluded, or a conflict exists) the tutelary judge makes the appointment (Arts. 343, 346 CC). Management of the inherited property: acts of extraordinary administration on the minors' real estate - sale, mortgage, lease over nine years - require authorisation of the tutelary judge (Arts. 320, para. 3, 374 and 375 CC), in voluntary jurisdiction. The earlier mediated separation settlement governs the separation only and does not bind the succession.

Reserved portion: with the spouse concurring with two (or more) children, the children are reserved 1/2 collectively and the spouse 1/4, leaving 1/4 disposable (Art. 542, para. 2 CC). Computation: take the relictum, deduct the debts, and add back (riunione fittizia) the value of the deceased's lifetime donations (Art. 556 CC); the reserved quotas are computed on this notional mass. Remedy: the forced heirs bring the action for reduction against the foundation (Arts. 553-564 CC), first reducing the testamentary disposition and then, if necessary, the donations, with restitution of the assets needed to fill the reserve (Art. 561 CC); the action is subject to the ten-year limitation. Role of mediation: disputes over hereditary division and successions are subject to mandatory mediation as a condition of admissibility of the claim (Art. 5 D.Lgs. 28/2010); family mediation/assisted negotiation can produce a settlement among the heirs and the foundation, but it cannot extinguish a forced heir's reserved right unless that heir consents.

Procedural sequence: (1) Because the legal community with the predeceased wife was never dissolved, it must first be liquidated (Art. 191 CC): the wife's half already devolved through her own succession, so the co-owned real estate is settled and apportioned before the father's estate is divided. (2) The father's succession opens; the two minor children are his sole intestate heirs in equal shares (Art. 566 CC). (3) A tutor is appointed for the minors by the tutelary judge, both parents being deceased (Arts. 343, 346 CC). (4) Acceptance for the minors is necessarily with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC). (5) The tutor petitions the tutelary judge for authorisation to accept with benefit of inventory (Art. 374, no. 3 CC) - voluntary jurisdiction. (6) The inventory is drawn up (Art. 484 CC). (7) Any subsequent act of extraordinary administration on the children's real estate requires further authorisation (Art. 375 CC).

Effect of the pending separation: a contested separation that has not been pronounced is extinguished by the husband's death, so the spouses are still married at the opening of the succession; the wife is a forced heir. (Only a final separation pronounced against her with a charge of fault would reduce her rights to a life annuity under Art. 548 CC.) Property regime: under the separation-of-property regime (Art. 215 CC) there is no community to divide, so the whole of the husband's own assets forms the relictum - no half is split off for the wife. Reserved share: the spouse not concurring with children is reserved 1/2 of the estate, with 1/2 disposable (Art. 540 CC). Remedies: the holographic will is valid if it meets Art. 602 CC; the wife may both contest its formal validity (Art. 606 CC) and, recovering her legittima, bring the action for reduction against the third-party heir (Arts. 553 ff. CC).

Steps: (1) Note that succession agreements are void (Art. 458 CC), so the 'mutual succession provisions' inserted in the assisted-negotiation agreement cannot bind the future succession; they have no effect on the devolution. (2) On the wife's intestate death the minor child is sole heir (Art. 566 CC); the separated/ex-spouse is not an heir. (3) Because the inheritance is burdened with debts, the protective course is acceptance with benefit of inventory (Art. 471 CC) to cap liability intra vires; outright renunciation for the minor is possible but only on the child's interest. (4) The father, exercising parental responsibility/as tutor, files a petition with the tutelary judge (voluntary jurisdiction) for authorisation to renounce (Arts. 320 and 374, no. 3 CC), showing the renunciation serves the minor's interest. (5) If authorised, the renunciation is declared before the notary or court clerk and entered in the succession register (Art. 519 CC).

Treatment of the conversion: a convention changing the matrimonial property regime is made by public deed before a notary (Arts. 162-163 CC) and, dissolving the legal community (Art. 191 CC), splits the community assets - including the family business - in equal halves between the spouses at the moment of conversion. Succession: at death the regime is separation of property, so the estate comprises only the deceased's own assets (his half of the former business and his other assets). The will may freely allocate the shares within the disposable quota, but the forced heirs (spouse and children) may raise the action for reduction (Arts. 553 ff. CC) if their reserved shares (Arts. 537 and 542 CC) are impaired; the reserve is computed on the relictum net of debts plus the notional reunion of donations (Art. 556 CC). A neutral 50/50 split on conversion is not a donation, but any conversion that disguised a liberality could itself be drawn into that computation.

Validity of the reciprocal will: Art. 589 CC prohibits joint and reciprocal wills - two persons cannot test in the same document, whether for a third party or reciprocally; such a will is void. If instead each spouse made a separate mirror will, each is individually valid. The succession provisions agreed in the assisted negotiation are also void as succession agreements (Art. 458 CC). Effect of separation: a consensual separation without a charge of fault does not remove spousal succession rights - the separated, non-faulted spouse inherits as a spouse (Art. 548 CC) - so the surviving spouse remains a forced heir. Reserved shares with spouse + one child: the child is reserved 1/3 and the spouse 1/3, leaving 1/3 disposable (Art. 542, para. 1 CC); the minor's reserved third is claimed through the tutor, with acceptance by benefit of inventory and the usual authorisations.
